### Purchase options and add-ons

* * *

Confucius (also known as Kong Qiu, and Kong Zhong Ni, 551 - 479 BC) was a prominent, if not the most influential, philosopher of China. His teachings have been fundamental to the Chinese civilization and culture for over two and half millennia. The Analects was written and compiled by Confucius' disciples after his death. It documented his conversations with his disciples and other relevant characters, and the dialogues among his disciples. These conversations highlighted key doctrines of Confucianism which cover a wide range of topics on education, self-cultivation, morality, ethics, society, social norms, government, law and order, politics, public service careers, music, poetry and so on. Many salient teachings of the Analects are still applicable today. Different from many translations, this new translation has deployed plain and colloquial English, simple writing style, and modern context to facilitate comprehension by common readers. This translation has also preserved accurately the ideas and tone of the original text. Annotations are added to further explain the background history, circumstances and the characters involved in the conversations documented in the Analects. This book will provide the reader many hours of enjoyable reading and inspirations for his or her self-cultivation.

## Editorial Reviews

### Review

BlueInk Editorial Review in November, 2020:Scholar Raymond K. Li has diligently constructed a modern translation of the words and teachings of Confucius (551- 479BC), one of the world's greatest philosophers. The Analects were originally compiled by Confucius's disciples after his death. For the Chinese, the work has been an enduring focal point for defining human character and purpose. In this new version, author Li uses contemporary English, including some needed annotations and definitions. In the first of 20 segments, Confucius extols the enjoyment of learning, introducing the noun Jun Zi, referring to a person of noble character, prominent, respectable and virtuous. Such a person would practice Ren, or core human virtues. Throughout the volume, Jun Zi individuals are cited for their positive qualities, and contrasted with their opposite, Xiao Ren, those who are mean, dishonest, etc. Li is a system of rites, ceremonies and protocols that underpin orderly life at all levels of society. A broad range of human behaviors and attitudes is expressed both by Confucius and his disciples. Their ideas sometimes exude a certain "Zen" element of surprise, as when Confucius asks students about their ambitions, and happily agrees most with one whose main dream involves dancing and singing "in the breeze and under the clouds." Yet the master can also be strict, suggesting, for example, that people should ceremoniously honor their deceased parents for three years, just as their parents cared for them the first three years of life. Li recommends reading the Analects slowly, with time for thought about the principles and incidents expressed. This will come naturally to readers as the concepts stimulate intellectually, intuitively, and emotionally. Repetition of words, concepts and individual names might become burdensome, but readers can easily skip over such portions. Particularly relevant are the observations regarding government and sound leadership, which will resonate in today's contentious political era. Overall, Li's prodigious efforts to translate Confucius to an English-speaking audience will be greatly appreciated at this tumultuous time in world history.  
  
 OnlineBookClub.org Official Review (4 out of 4 Stars): Confucius Analects , written by Raymond K. Li, is a collection of sayings and teachings attributed to Confucius and his disciples. The book details the most important thoughts and ideas of Confucius and also features several conversations between him, his disciples, and other notable people of their time. Originally written in Chinese, this new translation of Confucius Analects has been written in plain and simple English for the average reader. It also includes commentary and annotations that add much-needed context to some of the teachings. In sum, this book has been written with the aim of helping readers understand and apply the invaluable lessons of Confucianism in their lives. All in all, I learned so much from the teachings and quotes in the book, as I could relate them to my life. Often, I paused to ponder and reflect on the true meanings of the teachings, which were so inspiring that they have been imprinted on my mind. In fact, one of the most important lessons I took from the book is that one "should have internal substance as well as external sophistication." What this means is that we should not only strive to improve on our inner qualities but also our external appearance. Furthermore, I thoroughly enjoyed the commentary and annotations in the book, and without them, I dare say that I would have missed the true meanings of half of the teachings in the book. This shows just how important they were. The one thing I like most was the key lesson that I took from the book. It suggested that we should be pragmatic in life to change our ways according to prevailing circumstances and to avoid dogmatism. There was also a quote that God helps those who help themselves. I found this to be truly profound and insightful. Nonetheless, I had one issue with the book. After every quote, the author provided a short biography of every person mentioned and also a translation of every Chinese word present in the quote. Although I found them to be initially helpful, they quickly became tiresome and boring, as persons and Chinese words were consistently repeated throughout, accompanied by their short biographies and translations. I would have loved if if the author provided only one biography for every person and then referred the reader to the initial biography instead of repeating the same information whenever each person's name was mentioned. All things considered, I rate Confucius Analects 4 out of 4 stars . Indeed, my life has been profoundly impacted by the teachings in the book, and although I had one issue with it, I found the issue to be inadequate to affect the perfect rating. Further, I believe the book was exceptionally edited, as I found only one error in it. I would recommend it to anyone who loves reading self-improvement books.

### About the Author

Dr. Raymond K. Li, Ph.D., has earned advanced degrees in mathematics, economics, management, and finance. After six years in the academia, he has held senior management positions in a major multinational bank and firms in the Wall Street for 32 years. For the past 20 years, his interest has been switched to Chinese classics, oriental philosophies, and religions. He has tried to transcend his worldly experience and perspective of life in pursuit of spiritual enlightenment expounded by such philosophies and religions. Since his retirement, he has focused on the work of translating ancient Chinese classics into plain English so that common people in the West can understand and benefit from profound wisdom of ancient sages. Raymond Li is also the author of Sun Tzu's Military Principles, Applications to Business and Daily Life.

## Raymond K. Li

Brief content visible, double tap to read full content.

Full content visible, double tap to read brief content.

Dr. Raymond K. Li, Ph.D., has earned advanced degrees in mathematics, economics, management, and finance. After six years in the academia, he has held senior management positions in a major multinational bank and firms in the Wall Street for 32 years. For the past 20 years, his interest has been switched to Chinese classics, oriental philosophies, and religions. He has tried to transcend his worldly experience and perspective of life in pursuit of spiritual enlightenment expounded by such philosophies and religions. Since his retirement, he has focused on the work of translating and explaining ancient Chinese classics in plain English so that common readers in the West can understand and benefit from profound wisdom of ancient sages. He is the author of Confucius Analects, A New Translation with Annotations and Commentaries, Sun Tzu's Military Principles, Applications to Business and Daily Life, Mencius in Modern Perspectives, and The Life and Words of Confucius.

### Top reviews from the United States

#### There was a problem filtering reviews. Please reload the page.

* Charles J Arduini
  
  ##### _5\.0 out of 5 stars_ Highly Recommended
  
  Reviewed in the United States on November 21, 2021
  
  Format: Paperback Verified Purchase
  
  I had the pleasure to work with and for Raymond Li in the early 2000s. His generosity as a mentor and his wisdom as a teacher have been a blessing to me that I value still today. These traits of Raymond’s and his voice that I can still hear today come through on the written page with his translation of Confucius Analects. I strongly recommend this book for anyone looking for an exposition of the traditional, practical philosophy taught by Confucius. I found many of the chapters and sections highly applicable to many different areas of life’s circumstances. My favorite aspect of the translation was Raymond’s decision to leave terms such and Jun Zi and Ren untranslated. There are many aspects of these terms that would be lost with a simple translation to a single English word. I found the untranslated terms extremely helpful to bring me back to the multi-natured aspect of these desired attributes. As a Christian, it reminded me of the benefit in leaving agape untranslated in the New Testament instead of using a word such as love with its modern connotations and restriction in meaning. These decisions in translation along with many other delights in the work allow me to encourage all people to read this work.
  
  Read more
  
  One person found this helpful [Helpful](https://www.amazon.com/ap/signin?openid.return_to=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.amazon.com%2Fdp%2F1663200238%2Fref%3Dcm_cr_dp_d_vote_lft%3Fie%3DUTF8%26csrfT%3DhEAz7umwm9e9ysoXaepn38JZvdzC6JMHIWBZiiVhNXELAAAAAGnorGoAAAAB%26reviewId%3DR38GKSLYGO1DGU%23R38GKSLYGO1DGU&openid.identity=http%3A%2F%2Fspecs.openid.net%2Fauth%2F2.0%2Fidentifier_select&openid.assoc_handle=usflex&openid.mode=checkid_setup&marketPlaceId=ATVPDKIKX0DER&language=en&openid.claimed_id=http%3A%2F%2Fspecs.openid.net%2Fauth%2F2.0%2Fidentifier_select&openid.ns=http%3A%2F%2Fspecs.openid.net%2Fauth%2F2.0) Report
* Wackybidder
  
  ##### _5\.0 out of 5 stars_ A timely translation
  
  Reviewed in the United States on October 27, 2020
  
  Format: Paperback Verified Purchase
  
  Using simple, easy to understand English to explain even the most subtle concepts, this is an excellent introduction of the Analects to the general reading public. I especially appreciate the many background stories of Confucius’ pupils, most of which are unknown even to the modern Chinese readers.  
  More importantly, this is a timely publication to explain the founding philosophy that shapes much of the East Asian culture for the past two millennium and proves especially relevant to today’s societies.  
  As the COVID-19 pandemic sweeps across the United States and much of the world, we witness the dramatic differences in how the different societies and citizenry responded to this crisis, and their consequences. Especially jarring is the comparison between the United States and the East Asian countries. While these East Asian countries have vastly different forms of government, one common thread among them is the Confucius’ doctrine of balanced duties and mutual responsibilities between individuals and society. That balance enables the government and citizenry to work together to control the virus effectively. In the last few years, there are rising popularity with some extreme forms of individualism that resist any form of government restriction or regulation. Left unchecked, it will doom any attempt to solve the many intractable modern-day problems, including gun control, environmental degradation and climate change.

* MrSpeakEZ
  
  ##### _5\.0 out of 5 stars_ A must-have booklet on ethics and etiquette
  
  Reviewed in the United States on October 22, 2020
  
  Format: Paperback Verified Purchase
  
  Translation of Confucius Analects into an international language such as English is not only an altruistic mission but also a daunting semantic challenge. In the first place, it behooves the author to be genuinely proficient in classical Chinese literature, which was written in a style vastly different from contemporary Chinese. Whereas contemporary Chinese is unsophisticated, explicit and geared for the general public; classical Chinese is a more subtle and succinct language with an intended audience restricted to the literary intelligentsia. For those who can appreciate the beauty of classical literature, it is always a joy to read. However, such a pleasure may rapidly descend into a torment should one attempt to translate classical Chinese into a western language, whereupon disparities in culture and philosophy between the east and the west will present a hindrance. There are numerous instances where a matching English word or phrase that will work hand-in-glove with the Analects text does not exist. It is in these situations that Raymond Li, by applying his exceptional linguistic talent, proved himself to be a meritorious writer. In the end, he has produced a very readable English narrative which faithfully preserved the flavors and nuances of the original Chinese text. This book is recommended both for the novice reader of Confucius Analects as well as the seasoned scholar in Chinese literature.

* EM nights
  
  ##### _5\.0 out of 5 stars_ Great book!
  
  Reviewed in the United States on November 13, 2020
  
  Format: Paperback Verified Purchase
  
  I am a physician . I am an American of Chinese descent. I can speak the Chinese language but unable to read the characters. I bought this book for my father and he wantes me to share his review:  
    
  For many years, I have been looking for a good book on moral education and Chinese philosophy for my children and grandchildren. They know little about the Chinese language and far less about the Chinese culture and philosophy. Most books on this subject are difficult to understand and boring to read. After I finished a few chapters of Raymond Li’s book, I think it is a gem . This is exactly what I need. The contents of this book can teach my children and grand kids about some Chinese teachings.  
    
  I can find some valuable and inspiring quotes on every page of the book. Kudos to Dr. Li who has done a great job in making this important classic so easy to read and so easy to understand. I highly recommend this book to people who have children and grandchildren and want to teach them Chinese values.  
  K Y M

* Patrick Tse
  
  ##### _5\.0 out of 5 stars_ An insightful easy read
  
  Reviewed in the United States on December 13, 2020
  
  Format: Paperback Verified Purchase
  
  This an an excellent book for readers who do not read Chinese and yet want to learn about one of the great books of the Chinese culture. The original text in old literal Chinese is translated into easy to understand English which is followed by anecdotes of the author explaining the background of the person mentioned in the original text, its historical , cultural context as well as insight of the author. This adds a lot of color to the more traditional translations of this book which tend to be dry sometime. It is not necessary to read the book chronologically , one can pick up the book and read random pages and still gain an excellent insight to the lessons offered by Confucius. Another nice feature is the listing of the names of persons both in English and Chinese which is lacking is most English translation of this book.
